plannedCharts.title: India is running hotter than the climate it was built for
plannedCharts.subtitle: OWID / Copernicus ERA5 · annual temperature anomaly vs 1991-2020
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A viral death number means nothing until you know whether the heat behind it is real. It is: India's average year now runs well above the climate its cities, crops and hospitals were designed around.

plannedCharts.title: A warming century, one stripe per year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The same warming with no axis to argue with, the famous climate-stripes view: blue years gave way to red.

plannedCharts.title: Each decade has been hotter than the one before
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Smooth out the year-to-year noise and the warming becomes a staircase, not a wobble.

plannedCharts.title: The warming is not shared evenly across the map
plannedCharts.subtitle: Copernicus ERA5 · warming of 2015-2024 vs the 1951-1980 baseline, by state
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: One national number hides the geography, and India's heat lands unevenly: a single figure cannot tell a farmer in Telangana from a herder in Ladakh.

plannedCharts.title: And it is not only the heat, it is the humidity
plannedCharts.subtitle: Copernicus ERA5 · average relative humidity over India
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Dry heat the body can survive by sweating; humid heat is what kills, because sweat stops working when the air is already wet.

plannedCharts.title: Days that feel dangerously hot, now and to 2100
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank CCKP · CMIP6 · days with a heat index of 39°C or higher
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The heat index counts the days the air actually feels dangerous, temperature and humidity together. About 5 such days a year in the 1950s, 14 by 2014, and tens more by 2100 depending on how much the world keeps emitting.

plannedCharts.title: What actually happened after 2014, where the models stop
plannedCharts.subtitle: Copernicus ERA5 reanalysis · India-averaged days per year above threshold · 2015-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The model-based exposure record stops in 2014, which is exactly when the worst heat years arrived. This is the observed reanalysis filling that gap: every year since, an average Indian location has seen 70 to 85 warm nights and 10 to 30 dangerously hot days. Exposure did not pause when the projections took over, and the warm nights, when the body cannot recover, vastly outnumber the extreme hot days.

plannedCharts.title: The local heat the national average hides
plannedCharts.subtitle: Days per year above 35°C: average year in the 1940s versus the average year from 2016-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A national average is a fiction nobody lives in. Read this mainly as a burden chart, not a trend chart: in the latest decade, Jodhpur averaged nearly 150 days a year above 35°C, and Delhi, Lucknow, Varanasi, Jaipur and Nagpur were around or above 100. That is a long very-hot season, not a short heatwave. The 1940s bars are there as reference, because several interior cities were already brutally hot before recent warming pushed the wider climate system upward.

plannedCharts.title: And the nights are not cooling down either
plannedCharts.subtitle: Nights per year that stayed at or above 28°C, shown for metros and vulnerable-region city points on the same scale
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Hot nights are the hidden killer. A body can take a brutal afternoon if the night lets it cool and recover; when the night stays hot, the strain never lets up, and that is when the heart and kidneys give out. The split keeps two questions separate: what is happening in the big metros, and what the same metric looks like in city points from states the vulnerability section already flags.

plannedCharts.title: Heat is also a power bill and a grid problem
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank CCKP · CMIP6 · cooling degree days (base 18°C)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: More heat means more cooling, more electricity, more strain on the grid exactly when people most need a fan to survive the night. The danger and the power cut arrive together.

plannedCharts.title: The future, mapped: where dangerous heat lands by 2100
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank CCKP · CMIP6 · days a year with a heat index of 39°C or higher, 2080-2099 average, by state, under three emissions futures
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The line charts say how much hotter India gets; these three maps say where, and how much it depends on the choices the world makes now. Read left to right, low to high emissions, and watch the country darken. Under high emissions, much of the hot, humid plains and coasts spend more than half the year, up to about 200 days, in dangerous heat-index conditions by the end of the century.

plannedCharts.title: Most of India's workers are out in the heat
plannedCharts.subtitle: MoSPI · Periodic Labour Force Survey · share of workers by broad sector
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Heat exposure is decided at work. About 43% of India's workers are in agriculture, out in open fields, and much of industry is construction and brick kilns under the same sun. Only services, a minority of jobs, is mostly indoors. For most Indians, a heatwave is not something you can wait out at home.

plannedCharts.title: Most workers cannot simply stay home
plannedCharts.subtitle: MoSPI PLFS 2023-24 worker status + ILOSTAT informality, latest available
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The heat warning only helps if a worker can act on it. Most Indian workers cannot. PLFS says 58.4% of workers are self-employed and 19.8% are casual labour; only 21.7% have regular wage or salaried jobs. ILOSTAT puts informal employment at 87.2% overall and 98.6% in agriculture. That means no paid heat leave for most workers, no enforceable shade break, and no easy way to skip a dangerous day.

plannedCharts.title: Heat is already costing work hours
plannedCharts.subtitle: Lancet Countdown India 2025 · sector split of 247 billion potential labour hours lost in 2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The workplace burden is already visible in labour-capacity estimates. Lancet Countdown's India 2025 data sheet estimates heat exposure cost India 247 billion potential labour hours in 2024, 419 hours per person and 124% more than the 1990-1999 average. Agriculture accounts for 66% of those heat-related labour-hour losses, and construction for 20%, which is exactly where work is hardest to pause.

plannedCharts.title: Electricity reached the home. Cooling did not.
plannedCharts.subtitle: NFHS-6 electricity, NFHS-5 fan and AC/cooler, NSS 78 separate AC and cooler ownership
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This is the question behind every heat death: can people actually cool down? NFHS-6 says 98.3% of people live in households with electricity, and NFHS-5 says 88.3% of households own an electric fan. But the ladder drops quickly after that. Only 23.7% of households had an AC or cooler in NFHS-5; NSS 78 separates the appliances and finds 14.1% with an air cooler and just 4.9% with an air conditioner. The wire reached the home; reliable, effective cooling did not.

plannedCharts.title: The cooling map does not match the heat map
plannedCharts.subtitle: MoSPI · NSS 78th Round 2020-21 · households owning an air cooler, by state
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Where India keeps coolers is almost the opposite of where humid heat is most dangerous. The dry north, Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, Delhi and Chandigarh, runs on evaporative coolers, which work in dry air; the humid south and east, where wet-bulb heat is deadliest, own almost none because coolers barely work in damp air.

plannedCharts.title: And who can least afford to cope: India's poverty map
plannedCharts.subtitle: NITI Aayog National MPI 2023 · share of people who are multidimensionally poor (NFHS-5, 2019-21), by state
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Heat is only as deadly as your ability to escape it, and India is a poor country: about 15% of Indians are multidimensionally poor, deprived in housing, cooking fuel, assets or health. That poverty is concentrated in the east and centre, Bihar (34%), Jharkhand, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, exactly the populous, humid, heat-exposed plains. A tin-roof home with no reliable power and an undernourished body is what turns a hot week into a fatal one. For scale, the old global extreme-poverty line, $1.90 a day, is barely 150 rupees at today's exchange rate.

plannedCharts.title: Where monthly spending room is thinnest
plannedCharts.subtitle: MoSPI HCES 2023-24 · ten lowest rural MPCE states plus all-India rural average
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Poverty tells us deprivation; MPCE puts a rupee scale on the same heat problem. HCES 2023-24 shows the lowest rural monthly consumption is in Chhattisgarh (Rs 2,739 per person), Jharkhand (Rs 2,946), Odisha (Rs 3,357), Madhya Pradesh (Rs 3,441), Uttar Pradesh (Rs 3,481), West Bengal (Rs 3,620) and Bihar (Rs 3,670), against an all-India rural average of Rs 4,122. That is the thin monthly budget from which cooling, water, transport to care and lost work time must be absorbed.

plannedCharts.title: Heat hits a body that is often already weakened
plannedCharts.subtitle: NFHS-5 (2019-21) · share who are anaemic
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Heat does not kill at random. It finds bodies already under strain, and India's are: two in three young children and well over half of women are anaemic, which weakens the body's ability to handle heat stress. Add widespread high blood pressure, diabetes and kidney disease, and an ageing population, and the same heatwave that a healthy adult shrugs off can be fatal.

plannedCharts.title: The deadliest heat is humid, not just hot
plannedCharts.subtitle: CEEW · district Heat Risk Index 2025 · share of each state's districts rated high or very high risk
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Here is the twist this whole section has been building to. When CEEW folds heat, humidity, exposure and vulnerability into one risk index for all 734 districts, the places that top it are not the dry, record-breaking north, they are the humid south and coasts: Kerala, Goa, coastal Andhra Pradesh, all of Maharashtra. Wet heat stops the body cooling itself, so a humid 40C is deadlier than a dry 45C, and that is where the people are. 57% of districts, home to 76% of Indians, sit in the high or very high band.

plannedCharts.title: Where heat is hardest to survive
plannedCharts.subtitle: CEEW heat risk + NSS 78 cooling + NITI MPI + HCES MPCE · state-level context
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A heat-risk map tells you where the weather and vulnerability combine. A cooling chart tells you who can escape indoors. This scatter puts them together, with HCES MPCE carried into the tooltip and CSV as affordability context. States far to the right have many districts rated high or very high heat risk. States high up the chart have little AC/cooler protection by the conservative cooling proxy. Bigger bubbles are poorer states. The danger zone is the upper-right: high heat risk, little cooling, and often deeper poverty.

plannedCharts.title: So how many die? It depends entirely who you ask
plannedCharts.subtitle: Curated from Frontiers, OWID/EM-DAT, NCRB, IMD and NCDC reporting · log scale
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Here is the heart of the confusion. The viral 3,400 is a modelled estimate of extra deaths on an extreme day; the small official numbers are head-counts of deaths formally labelled as heatstroke. They are not rival versions of one fact.

plannedCharts.title: Most deaths are registered. Most causes are not medically certified.
plannedCharts.subtitle: CRS 2023 + MCCD 2023 · death registration, medical attention and cause certification
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This is the missing plumbing behind the heat-death fight. India registers most deaths, but only a small share occur in institutions and only about a fifth get medically certified causes. Heat can push a weak heart, kidney or lung over the edge and never appear as heat on the record.

plannedCharts.title: Where causes of death are least visible
plannedCharts.subtitle: MCCD 2023 · medically certified deaths as a share of registered deaths, by state
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The undercount is geographical too. In Maharashtra, around 42% of registered deaths are medically certified by cause; in Uttar Pradesh, Assam and Bihar it is about 6%. A heat death is easiest to miss where the cause-of-death system is thinnest.

plannedCharts.title: Where India officially counted its heat deaths in 2024
plannedCharts.subtitle: IMD Disastrous Weather Events 2024 · Table 22 · reported heatwave deaths by state
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: When the counting works at all, it points at the same plains the warming map flagged: Uttar Pradesh alone reported more than half of 2024's official heatwave deaths.

plannedCharts.title: Heat kills in a tight, predictable window
plannedCharts.subtitle: IMD Disastrous Weather Events 2024 · Table 7 · reported heatwave deaths by month
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The official deaths cluster in May and June, the pre-monsoon furnace, which is exactly why they are foreseeable and, in principle, preventable.

plannedCharts.title: In 2015 India counted 2,000 heat deaths. In 2021, zero.
plannedCharts.subtitle: IMD Disastrous Weather Events · reported heatwave deaths · 2013 to 2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This single line is the strongest proof that India does not really know its heat toll. The same agency recorded over 2,000 heat deaths in 2015 and exactly zero in 2021. The sun did not take a year off. What changed was attention and bookkeeping: after the catastrophic 2015 heatwave, states launched Heat Action Plans that genuinely saved lives, but the recorded number also collapsed because no one was systematically counting, and it can fall even in years when the heat is rising. The official figure measures how hard India is looking, not how many die.

plannedCharts.title: Why the disaster databases always look too small
plannedCharts.subtitle: OWID / EM-DAT · India reported extreme-temperature disaster deaths since 2000
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: International disaster databases catch the big, named heatwaves and miss the slow burn, so the long record is spiky and almost certainly an undercount of heat's real toll.

plannedCharts.title: How a single hot day can plausibly reach four figures
plannedCharts.subtitle: Illustrative denominator model using 2024 population and a rounded crude death rate · not attribution
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This is the test of the viral number. India loses roughly 26,000 people a day no matter the weather, so even a small temporary lift in that baseline, spread across a huge exposed population, adds up to thousands. That makes 3,400 plausible, not proven.

plannedCharts.title: The number any honest estimate has to start from
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · crude death rate, deaths per 1,000 population
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Every excess-death claim, viral or rigorous, begins by asking how many people would have died anyway. Get that baseline wrong and the whole heat number is wrong.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
